abstract:
In this review, the properties and synthetic utility of azomethine imines as versatile 1,3-dipoles are examined, focusing on recent developments in the construction of dinitrogenated heterocycles. Classified as aza-allyl type intermediates, these dipoles exist as either transient species generated in situ or as stable, isolable compounds. While they predominantly undergo [3 + 2] cycloadditions with various alkenes, alkynes, and allenoates, they are also capable of participating in higher-order annulations, including [3 + 3], [4 + 3], [5 + 3], and [3 + 2 + 2] processes.

The discussion covers a broad structural range of dipoles, from acyclic versions derived from hydrazones to cyclic systems such as isoquinolinium imides and N-alkylidene-3-oxo-pyrazolidinium ylides. Significant attention is given to the evolution of catalytic methods, highlighting how thermal and microwave-assisted reactions have been augmented by advanced metallo- and organocatalytic systems. In particular, the review emphasizes the growth of enantioselective catalysis, which has enabled the precise synthesis of biologically active molecules, pharmaceuticals, and agrochemicals. By summarizing the literature from the past decade, this paper provides a comprehensive overview of the chemical behavior of azomethine imines and their expanding role in modern organic synthesis.
